prompts.title: RCC Singly Reinforced Beam Design (IS 456 LSM)
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior RCC design consultant with 15 years of experience designing residential and commercial buildings in Mumbai and Pune. I need you to design a singly reinforced rectangular RCC beam as per IS 456:2000 using Limit State Method. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil engineering student working on my Design of Concrete Structures assignment. My beam has a clear span of [ENTER SPAN in m], simply supported on 230 mm thick masonry walls, carrying a dead load of [ENTER DL in kN/m] (excluding self-weight) and a live load of [ENTER LL in kN/m]. Concrete grade is M25 and steel is Fe500. The beam is inside a building so mild exposure applies. Please provide: (1) effective span calculation with sketch explanation, (2) trial cross-section (b and D) with rationale based on span/depth ratio, (3) factored load and design BM/SF with load combination 1.5(DL+LL), (4) check whether section is under-reinforced, balanced, or over-reinforced using Mu,lim formula, (5) area of tension steel Ast with bar selection (number and diameter) and spacing check, (6) shear design with stirrup spacing (Vus, tau_c from Table 19), (7) development length Ld check at support, (8) deflection check using modification factors from Cl. 23.2.1, (9) crack width/minimum steel check. Format the output as a step-by-step calculation sheet with IS 456 clause references at every step, followed by a final summary box. Consider these constraints: use only IS 456:2000 and SP-16 design aids, follow Indian practice of 25 mm nominal cover for mild exposure, and flag anything that would fail a university viva.

prompts.title: Terzaghi Bearing Capacity for Shallow Footing
prompts.prompt: Act as a geotechnical engineer with 12 years of experience working on foundation design for projects across Delhi NCR and Bengaluru. I need you to compute the safe bearing capacity of a shallow strip/square footing using Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation and cross-verify with IS 6403. Here is the context: I am a civil engineering student preparing for my Foundation Engineering end-sem and GATE. The footing is [square/strip — ENTER TYPE] with width B = [ENTER B in m], placed at depth Df = [ENTER Df in m] below ground level. Soil is [c-phi soil] with cohesion c = [ENTER c in kN/m2], angle of internal friction phi = [ENTER phi in degrees], and bulk unit weight gamma = [ENTER gamma in kN/m3]. Water table is at [ENTER water table depth] below GL. Please provide: (1) the governing Terzaghi equation for the footing type with each term explained, (2) bearing capacity factors Nc, Nq, Ngamma looked up for the given phi (state source table), (3) water table correction factors Rw1 and Rw2 with the case identified (Case I, II, or III), (4) ultimate bearing capacity qu, net ultimate qnu, and net safe bearing capacity qns using FoS = 3, (5) safe bearing pressure qs including overburden, (6) a brief comparison of how answer would differ using Meyerhof's equation, (7) common GATE-style traps for this problem. Format the output as a worked numerical solution with boxed final answers in kN/m2 and a short Viva Q&A section at the end. Consider these constraints: assume general shear failure unless phi < 28, use IS 6403:1981 terminology, and round unit weights per Indian textbook conventions (Arora/Punmia).

prompts.title: Dead and Live Load Calculation per IS 875
prompts.prompt: Act as a structural engineer with 10 years of experience at a mid-size consultancy in Chennai working on G+4 residential buildings. I need you to help me compute dead loads and live loads on a typical floor slab and beam as per IS 875 Part 1 and Part 2. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student doing my Building Planning and Design mini-project. My typical floor plan has a room of size [ENTER Lx x Ly in m], 125 mm thick RCC slab, 20 mm cement plaster on ceiling, 40 mm floor finish (cement screed + vitrified tile), 115 mm brick partition wall 3 m high running along one beam, and the usage is [residential/office/classroom]. Please provide: (1) unit weight table from IS 875 Part 1 for RCC, brick, plaster, floor finish with source page, (2) step-by-step dead load per m2 of slab broken into components, (3) equivalent UDL on the supporting beam from slab DL using one-way or two-way distribution (state which and why using Lx/Ly), (4) wall load on beam as UDL in kN/m, (5) live load value from IS 875 Part 2 Table 1 for the given usage with clause reference, (6) total factored load 1.5(DL+LL) on the beam, (7) a clean load takedown diagram described in text. Format the output as a tabulated load calculation sheet followed by a summary of total UDL on beam in kN/m. Consider these constraints: follow IS 875 strictly, treat partition load as per Cl. 3.1.2 of Part 2 if not shown on plan, and state assumptions clearly at the top.

prompts.title: Bar Bending Schedule for RCC Beam
prompts.prompt: Act as a site engineer with 8 years of experience at L&T Construction handling RCC works for high-rise residential projects in Hyderabad. I need you to prepare a complete Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) for a simply supported RCC beam. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student submitting a practical training report and I need a BBS that would be accepted on a real L&T site. Beam dimensions: width [ENTER b in mm], overall depth [ENTER D in mm], clear span [ENTER L in mm], supported on 230 mm walls both ends, clear cover 25 mm. Reinforcement details: [ENTER main bars bottom, e.g., 3-16 mm Fe500], [ENTER top anchor bars, e.g., 2-12 mm], stirrups [ENTER dia, e.g., 8 mm @ 150 c/c]. Please provide: (1) a clean BBS table with columns: Bar Mark, Dia, Shape Code (as per BS 8666 / Indian practice), No. of bars, Cutting Length, Total Length, Unit Weight (kg/m), Total Weight, (2) cutting length calculation for each bar type showing hook/bend deductions (90 deg bend = 2d, 135 deg = 3d, hook = 9d as per IS 2502), (3) stirrup cutting length with the 2(a+b) + hooks formula, (4) total steel weight in kg with 3% wastage, (5) a short note on lap length using 50d for Fe500 in M25, (6) a mild-steel vs TMT comparison note. Format the output as a Markdown table BBS followed by calculation workings and a final weight summary. Consider these constraints: use IS 2502 for hook/bend allowances, unit weights d^2/162 for round bars, and flag any bar that would need coupler instead of lap for real L&T QA/QC.

prompts.title: Fly Levelling Traverse Reduction (HI and Rise-Fall)
prompts.prompt: Act as a surveying lecturer with 20 years of experience teaching at an Indian engineering college and running field practicals with auto levels and dumpy levels. I need you to reduce a differential/fly levelling field book using both the Height of Instrument method and the Rise and Fall method, and arithmetically check both. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student submitting my Surveying-1 practical record. Here are my field readings from a closed loop starting at BM RL [ENTER RL in m]: [ENTER station-wise BS, IS, FS readings as a list, e.g., Stn A BS 1.235; Stn B IS 0.985, IS 2.145; Stn C FS 1.875 BS 0.765; ...]. Please provide: (1) a fully filled HI method table with columns Station, BS, IS, FS, HI, RL, Remarks, (2) the same data reduced by Rise and Fall method with columns Station, BS, IS, FS, Rise, Fall, RL, Remarks, (3) arithmetic check for HI method (sum BS - sum FS = last RL - first RL), (4) arithmetic check for Rise-Fall method (sum BS - sum FS = sum Rise - sum Fall = last RL - first RL), (5) closing error and permissible error as 25*sqrt(K) mm for ordinary levelling (state K in km), (6) distribution of closing error proportionally to number of setups, (7) final corrected RLs. Format the output as two neatly aligned Markdown tables followed by the checks and a short remarks paragraph. Consider these constraints: use Indian textbook conventions (Punmia/Arora), show intermediate sights correctly (only HI method handles IS directly), and flag any reading that looks like a booking error.

prompts.title: One-Way Slab Design as per IS 456
prompts.prompt: Act as a principal structural engineer with 18 years of experience designing slabs for Shapoorji Pallonji housing projects in Mumbai. I need you to design a simply supported one-way RCC slab as per IS 456:2000. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student. Slab details: clear span Lx = [ENTER shorter span in m], Ly = [ENTER longer span in m], supported on 230 mm walls all sides. Confirm one-way behaviour by checking Ly/Lx > 2. Concrete M25, steel Fe500, live load [ENTER LL in kN/m2], floor finish 1.5 kN/m2, exposure mild. Please provide: (1) one-way vs two-way check with Ly/Lx ratio, (2) assumed depth from Cl. 23.2.1 span/depth = 20 x modification factor, trial D and effective d with 15 mm cover + half bar dia, (3) effective span as per Cl. 22.2, (4) loads per m2 including self-weight, total w and factored wu, (5) design BM Mu = wu.Lx^2/8 per m width, (6) depth required from Mu,lim and check against provided d, (7) main steel Ast per m and bar spacing check against 3d or 300 mm, (8) distribution steel 0.12% for Fe500 with spacing check against 5d or 450 mm, (9) shear check Vu and tau_v vs tau_c from Table 19 with k factor for slabs, (10) deflection check, (11) a neat reinforcement detail sketch described in text (alternate bent-up bars or straight bars). Format the output as a structured calculation sheet with clause refs and a final summary table. Consider these constraints: use limit state method only, Fe500 minimum steel 0.12%, and add a note on how detailing changes if continuous over support.

prompts.title: Concrete Mix Design M25 per IS 10262
prompts.prompt: Act as a concrete technologist with 14 years of experience in ready-mix plants supplying UltraTech and ACC concrete to DLF projects in Gurgaon. I need you to do a complete M25 nominal mix design using IS 10262:2019. Here is the context: I am a civil engineering student doing my Concrete Technology lab assignment. Design requirements: grade M25, OPC 53 grade (specific gravity 3.15), maximum nominal aggregate size [ENTER 20/10 mm], workability 75-100 mm slump, exposure moderate, method of placing pumping, good degree of quality control. Fine aggregate Zone II (SG 2.65, absorption 1%), coarse aggregate (SG 2.74, absorption 0.5%). Admixture: PCE superplasticiser at 0.8% by weight of cement, water reduction 18%. Please provide: (1) target mean strength fck + 1.65 x S (state S from Table 2 of IS 10262), (2) selection of w/c ratio with check against Table 5 of IS 456 maximum for moderate exposure, (3) selection of water content from Table 4 with correction for slump and superplasticiser, (4) cement content with minimum check for moderate exposure (300 kg/m3), (5) volume of coarse aggregate from Table 5 with correction for w/c, (6) mix calculations by absolute volume method to get mass of CA and FA per m3, (7) trial mix proportions in kg/m3 and ratio by weight, (8) adjustment for field moisture, (9) a short note on expected 7-day and 28-day strength. Format the output as a step-by-step design sheet matching the IS 10262 Annex A worked example layout. Consider these constraints: use only IS 10262:2019 (not 2009), quote all table numbers, and state any assumption explicitly.

prompts.title: Two-Way Slab Design (Restrained) using IS 456 Annex D
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior RCC consultant with 16 years of experience designing floor systems for Tata Projects commercial buildings in Bengaluru. I need you to design a two-way restrained RCC slab using IS 456 Annex D coefficients. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student. Panel: Lx = [ENTER shorter span in m], Ly = [ENTER longer span in m], Ly/Lx <= 2, edge condition [ENTER one of 9 cases from Table 26 of IS 456, e.g., Case 4 two adjacent edges discontinuous]. Concrete M25, steel Fe500, live load [ENTER LL kN/m2], floor finish 1.5 kN/m2, exposure mild. Please provide: (1) trial depth from span/depth ratio using modification factor, (2) effective spans and loads (DL+LL factored), (3) alpha_x and alpha_y moment coefficients for negative and positive moments from Table 26 for the given edge case, (4) design moments Mux+, Mux-, Muy+, Muy-, (5) depth check with Mu,lim at the worst section, (6) main steel Astx and Asty for middle strip with bar selection and spacing, (7) steel at continuous edges (top bars) with curtailment per Cl. D-1.4, (8) torsion reinforcement at corners per Cl. D-1.8 with the 3/4 Ast rule for non-continuous corners, (9) shear check, (10) deflection check with modification factor for tension steel, (11) a detailing note on middle strip vs edge strip. Format the output as an ordered calculation sheet with boxed answers and a summary reinforcement table. Consider these constraints: strictly follow IS 456 Annex D and Cl. 24.4, use Fe500 minimum steel 0.12%, and flag if slab fails deflection.

prompts.title: Axially Loaded Short RCC Column Design
prompts.prompt: Act as an RCC design engineer with 12 years of experience at a Kolkata consultancy designing columns for G+15 residential towers. I need you to design a short axially loaded RCC column as per IS 456 Cl. 39. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student. Column carries a factored axial load Pu = [ENTER Pu in kN], unsupported length [ENTER L in m], effectively held in position and restrained against rotation at both ends. Concrete M25, steel Fe500, cover 40 mm. Please provide: (1) slenderness check using effective length from Table 28 of IS 456 to confirm 'short' column (lex/D and ley/b < 12), (2) minimum eccentricity check ex,min = L/500 + D/30 >= 20 mm and whether Cl. 39.3 can be used directly, (3) design equation Pu = 0.4 fck Ac + 0.67 fy Asc, (4) trial column size, (5) steel percentage pt with limits 0.8% to 6% from Cl. 26.5.3.1, (6) bar selection (even number, symmetrical), (7) lateral ties design: diameter max(6 mm, dia/4), pitch min of least lateral dimension, 16 x longitudinal dia, 300 mm, (8) development length check at base, (9) a detailing sketch described in text with cover and tie arrangement. Format the output as a calculation sheet followed by a section summary. Consider these constraints: use IS 456 Cl. 25 and 26.5.3 strictly, Fe500, and add a sentence on how design changes if minimum eccentricity exceeds 0.05D (becomes uniaxial bending).

prompts.title: Isolated Square Footing Design per IS 456
prompts.prompt: Act as a foundation engineer with 15 years of experience designing shallow foundations for Prestige Group apartments in Bengaluru on weathered granite. I need you to design an isolated square RCC footing. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. Column size [ENTER b x D in mm] carrying service axial load P = [ENTER P in kN] (no moment), safe bearing capacity of soil qs = [ENTER qs in kN/m2] at 1.5 m depth. Concrete M25, steel Fe500, cover 50 mm for footings. Please provide: (1) footing plan size from P + 10% self weight / qs with check for square, (2) factored net upward pressure pu on footing, (3) bending moment at face of column Mu = pu x L x (L-b)^2 / 8 per m or the correct formulation for square footing, (4) depth required from Mu,lim check, (5) one-way (wide beam) shear check at d from column face with tau_c from Table 19, (6) two-way (punching) shear check at d/2 from column face against ks x 0.25 x sqrt(fck), (7) main reinforcement Ast both ways with bar selection and spacing, (8) check for uniform distribution across full width (for square footing all in band), (9) development length check Ld from column face, (10) a neat reinforcement layout described in text. Format the output as a structured calculation sheet with clause references and a boxed final design summary (plan size, depth, steel). Consider these constraints: follow IS 456 Cl. 34, minimum steel 0.12% for Fe500, and note when footing depth is governed by punching shear vs moment.

prompts.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams
prompts.prompt: Act as a structural analysis tutor with 10 years of experience coaching students for GATE Civil and ESE. I need you to draw shear force and bending moment diagrams for a statically determinate beam and mark all salient values. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student preparing for my Strength of Materials end-sem and GATE. Beam type and loading: [ENTER description, e.g., simply supported beam 6 m with point load 20 kN at 2 m from left, UDL 10 kN/m over right 3 m, and a concentrated moment 15 kNm anticlockwise at midspan]. Please provide: (1) free body diagram with all forces and support reactions labelled, (2) reactions Ra and Rb using sum(Fy)=0 and sum(M)=0 with working, (3) shear force equations for each segment with sign convention (upward positive on left face), (4) SF values at every critical section (just left and right of each discrete load), (5) location of zero shear (for maximum BM), (6) bending moment equations for each segment, (7) BM values at all salient points including maximum, (8) point of contraflexure if any (M = 0 where slope changes), (9) a described SFD and BMD with values annotated on the diagrams, (10) a one-line sanity check using sum of loads equals sum of reactions. Format the output as a worked solution with clear segmented calculations and an ASCII-art or text-described SFD/BMD. Consider these constraints: use kN and kNm, follow standard Indian textbook sign convention (sagging positive BM), and flag the absolute maximum BM with its location in meters from left support.

prompts.title: Theodolite Traverse Adjustment by Bowditch Rule
prompts.prompt: Act as a survey engineer with 18 years of field experience running traverses for highway alignment surveys in Maharashtra and Gujarat. I need you to balance a closed theodolite traverse using Bowditch's rule and compute the coordinates of all stations. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student submitting my Surveying-2 practical record. Here are my field observations for a closed traverse ABCDA: [ENTER for each line: line AB length in m, whole circle bearing in deg-min-sec]. Starting coordinates of A: N 1000.000, E 1000.000. Please provide: (1) a table of lines with length, reduced bearing, latitude (L cos theta) and departure (L sin theta), (2) sum of latitudes and departures with the closing errors eL and eD, (3) linear closing error sqrt(eL^2 + eD^2) and direction, (4) relative precision as 1 in (perimeter/closing error) with acceptability check (typical 1:1000 to 1:5000), (5) corrections to each latitude and departure by Bowditch rule (correction proportional to length), (6) corrected latitudes and departures, (7) independent coordinates (N, E) of each station computed progressively, (8) a final closure check that returns to start, (9) a short comparison note on Transit rule vs Bowditch rule applicability. Format the output as aligned Markdown tables for each step, with all bearings converted to reduced bearings and quadrants shown. Consider these constraints: use Indian conventions (WCB from north clockwise), show angles in deg-min-sec, and round coordinates to 3 decimal places.

prompts.title: Quantity Estimation for G+1 Residential Building
prompts.prompt: Act as a quantity surveyor with 20 years of experience working on CPWD and PWD residential projects in Lucknow and Jaipur. I need you to prepare a detailed quantity estimate for a G+1 residential building using the long wall-short wall (centre line) method. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my Estimation and Costing project. Building plan: [ENTER plinth area L x B in m], external wall thickness 230 mm, internal partition 115 mm, [ENTER number of rooms and internal wall layout]. Foundation type isolated column footings / strip footing [ENTER], plinth height 600 mm, floor-to-floor height 3.0 m, roof RCC slab 125 mm, M25 grade. Please provide: (1) assumptions and plan description, (2) centre-line length calculation for walls, (3) earthwork in excavation in m3 (length x breadth x depth), (4) PCC 1:4:8 bed in m3, (5) size stone/RR masonry in foundation in m3, (6) brickwork in superstructure in m3 split for external and internal, (7) RCC for columns, beams, slab, lintels in m3, (8) plastering inside and outside in m2, (9) flooring in m2, (10) painting and whitewash in m2, (11) doors and windows as numbers, (12) a final abstract of quantities table. Format the output as an item-wise measurement sheet (No, Description, No, L, B, H/D, Quantity, Unit) followed by an abstract. Consider these constraints: use CPWD Mode of Measurement, deduct openings as per IS 1200, and state any assumption about door/window sizes (e.g., D1 1.0 x 2.1 m, W1 1.2 x 1.2 m).

prompts.title: Rate Analysis for 1:2:4 PCC using DSR
prompts.prompt: Act as a billing engineer with 12 years of experience preparing rate analyses for CPWD and MES projects in Delhi. I need you to prepare a detailed rate analysis for 1 cubic metre of Plain Cement Concrete 1:2:4 using current CPWD DSR methodology. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Estimation lab. Use the following rates (update to current DSR if needed but show my inputs): cement OPC 53 at Rs [ENTER per bag of 50 kg], coarse sand at Rs [ENTER per m3], coarse aggregate 20 mm at Rs [ENTER per m3], mason (1st class) at Rs [ENTER per day], mazdoor at Rs [ENTER per day], bhisti at Rs [ENTER per day]. Please provide: (1) materials calculation with dry volume factor 1.54 applied to 1 m3 wet volume, cement, sand, aggregate quantities using 1+2+4=7 parts method, (2) cement in bags (1 bag = 0.0347 m3 or 50 kg / 1440 kg/m3), (3) material cost sub-total, (4) labour constants per m3 as per CPWD DSR (mason, mazdoor, bhisti, water carrier), (5) labour cost sub-total, (6) sundries 1%, water charges 1%, tools and plant 1%, (7) contractor's profit 10% and overheads 5%, (8) GST 18% treatment note (whether included in rate or added separately per CPWD), (9) final rate per m3 in INR. Format the output as a standard rate analysis sheet with material, labour, sundries and total sections clearly separated. Consider these constraints: follow CPWD Analysis of Rates methodology, state DSR version used, and flag how the rate changes if M15 design mix is used instead of nominal 1:2:4.

prompts.title: Standard Penetration Test (SPT) Correction and Interpretation
prompts.prompt: Act as a geotechnical investigation engineer with 14 years of experience conducting borehole investigations for metro projects in Mumbai and Chennai. I need you to correct raw SPT N values for overburden pressure and dilatancy, and interpret the soil profile. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student working on a geotechnical mini-project. Borehole log data: [ENTER depth in m, soil type, unit weight gamma, and field N value for each 1.5 m stratum, e.g., 1.5 m silty sand gamma 18 kN/m3 N=8; 3.0 m sand gamma 19 N=15; ...]. Water table at [ENTER depth] m. Please provide: (1) effective overburden pressure sigma'v at each SPT depth with correct unit weights above/below water table, (2) overburden correction factor CN using the IS 2131 / Liao-Whitman formula, (3) N1 = CN x N, (4) dilatancy correction for saturated fine sands/silts: N' = 15 + 0.5(N-15) if N > 15, (5) corrected final (N1)60 equivalent values, (6) a soil profile table showing depth, soil, gamma, N field, N corrected, (7) correlations to relative density (Terzaghi-Peck) and phi angle (Peck correlation), (8) a bearing capacity recommendation based on corrected N using Teng's or Meyerhof's N-based formula for shallow footings at 1.5 m depth and 2 m width, (9) consistency/density classification per IS 1498. Format the output as a tabular borehole log followed by interpretation notes. Consider these constraints: follow IS 2131:1981 and IS 1892 for sampling, clearly state which correction applies in which order, and note when SPT is unreliable (clays, gravels).

prompts.title: Highway Geometric Design: Horizontal Curve per IRC 73
prompts.prompt: Act as a highway design engineer with 15 years of experience designing NH and SH projects for NHAI and MoRTH in Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh. I need you to design a horizontal circular curve with transition for a two-lane rural highway as per IRC 73 and IRC SP 23. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student. Given: design speed V = [ENTER km/h], radius of curve R = [ENTER R in m] (or allow me to compute minimum R), terrain [plain/rolling/mountainous], pavement width 7 m, carriageway single two-way, coefficient of lateral friction f = 0.15, maximum superelevation 7% (plain/rolling). Please provide: (1) minimum radius check from V^2 / 127(e+f), (2) superelevation design: first by e = V^2/225R ignoring friction, check against e_max, if > e_max design by increasing f to 0.15 and restricting V, (3) length of transition curve by three methods (rate of change of centrifugal acceleration, rate of introduction of superelevation pavement rotation about centre line, empirical IRC 2.7 V^2/R) and take the maximum, (4) set-back distance for sight distance using SSD, (5) extra widening = nl^2/2R + V/9.5 sqrt(R), (6) shift S = Ls^2/24R, (7) total tangent length and length of circular curve, (8) chainages at TS, SC, CS, ST given PI chainage [ENTER PI chainage] and deflection angle [ENTER delta in degrees]. Format the output as an IRC-style design sheet with each parameter boxed. Consider these constraints: strictly follow IRC 73:1980 and IRC SP 23, use SSD from IRC 66, and state units clearly (m, km/h).

prompts.title: CBR Test Pavement Thickness Design per IRC 37
prompts.prompt: Act as a flexible pavement design engineer with 12 years of experience at an NHAI consultancy designing overlays and new pavements for rural roads under PMGSY. I need you to design a flexible pavement thickness using the CBR method and the IRC 37:2018 catalogue. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. Design inputs: initial traffic in year of completion = [ENTER CVPD], traffic growth rate r = 5%, design life n = [ENTER years, typically 15 for NH], lane distribution factor 0.75 (two-lane two-way), vehicle damage factor VDF = [ENTER VDF e.g., 3.5 for plain terrain], subgrade CBR = [ENTER CBR %] (soaked, 4 days). Please provide: (1) cumulative standard axles (msa) using the IRC 37 formula N = 365 x A x (1+r)^x x [(1+r)^n - 1]/r x D x F, (2) design traffic category (5-10 msa, 10-20 msa etc.), (3) pavement composition recommendation from IRC 37 Plate/Table for given CBR and msa (BC + DBM + WMM + GSB thicknesses in mm), (4) check total thickness against minimum crust, (5) alternative using mechanistic-empirical (IIT Pave) with elastic moduli note, (6) subgrade preparation and quality requirements (density 97% MDD, CBR after compaction), (7) drainage layer requirement, (8) expected fatigue and rutting life note. Format the output as a design summary sheet with a layer-by-layer pavement section described in text. Consider these constraints: use IRC 37:2018 only, state Plate number used, and note minimum CBR of 8% for design of new roads per IRC.

prompts.title: Moment Distribution Method for Continuous Beam
prompts.prompt: Act as a structural analysis professor with 22 years of experience teaching indeterminate structures at an Indian NIT. I need you to analyse a continuous beam by Hardy Cross Moment Distribution Method. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student preparing for my Structural Analysis-2 end-sem. Beam: [ENTER span configuration, e.g., three spans ABCD with AB = 4 m, BC = 6 m, CD = 5 m], supports [ENTER fixed/roller conditions], EI [constant/varying]. Loading: [ENTER span-wise loads, e.g., AB UDL 20 kN/m, BC point load 40 kN at midspan, CD UDL 15 kN/m]. Please provide: (1) fixed end moments for each span using standard formulas (wl^2/12 for UDL, Pl/8 for central point load, etc.) with signs (clockwise positive on joint), (2) stiffness k = 4EI/L for each member with modification 3EI/L for far-end hinged, (3) distribution factors at each joint (k/sum k), (4) carry over factor 0.5 (or 0 for hinged far end), (5) full moment distribution table with multiple cycles until balanced to within 1%, (6) final end moments, (7) span shears and support reactions, (8) SFD and BMD sketched in text with values, (9) a one-line check that sum of support moments satisfies equilibrium. Format the output as a moment distribution table with columns per member end, iterations per row, and a final moments summary. Consider these constraints: use Indian textbook (Vazirani-Ratwani/Reddy) sign convention, show at least 5 balancing cycles, and flag where near-symmetry allows shortcut using symmetry/antisymmetry.

prompts.title: Consolidation Settlement of Clay Layer (Terzaghi 1D)
prompts.prompt: Act as a geotechnical engineer with 16 years of experience predicting settlements for high-rise foundations on Mumbai marine clay and Kolkata clay. I need you to compute the primary consolidation settlement of a saturated normally consolidated clay layer using Terzaghi's one-dimensional theory. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. Soil profile: top 2 m sand gamma = 18 kN/m3, water table at 2 m, then saturated sand gamma_sat = 19 kN/m3 up to 5 m, then saturated NC clay layer of thickness H = [ENTER H in m] with gamma_sat = 17 kN/m3, compression index Cc = [ENTER Cc], initial void ratio e0 = [ENTER e0], then hard stratum. A uniform surcharge of delta_sigma = [ENTER in kPa] is applied at ground surface (treat as 1D). Please provide: (1) effective overburden sigma'0 at mid-depth of clay layer with correct gamma' below water table, (2) stress increment delta sigma' at mid-depth (1D so unchanged with depth), (3) primary consolidation settlement Sc = (Cc x H)/(1+e0) x log10((sigma'0 + delta sigma')/sigma'0), (4) time for 90% consolidation using Tv = 0.848 for double drainage if hard stratum impermeable (single drainage) or permeable (double drainage) — state assumption, t = Tv x Hd^2 / Cv with [ENTER Cv in m2/year], (5) settlement at 1 year, 5 years, end of construction, (6) check if OC (use Cr/Cc ratio) and how equation changes, (7) a short note on secondary compression (Calpha) for organic clay, (8) practical mitigation (preloading, PVD/wick drains). Format the output as a stepwise calculation with boxed Sc and t90. Consider these constraints: use IS 8009 Part 1, state drainage boundary clearly, and convert Cv units carefully (cm2/sec to m2/year).

prompts.title: Steel Tension Member Design per IS 800
prompts.prompt: Act as a steel structures engineer with 14 years of experience designing industrial sheds and transmission towers for BHEL and Tata Steel projects. I need you to design a steel tension member using IS 800:2007 Limit State Method. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Design of Steel Structures assignment. Tension member carries factored axial tension Tu = [ENTER Tu in kN], length between connections = [ENTER L in m], connected at ends using [ENTER bolted/welded] joint. Preferred section: [ENTER ISA single angle / double angle / ISMB]. Steel grade Fe410 (fy = 250, fu = 410 MPa). Please provide: (1) approximate gross area required Ag = Tu x 1000 / (fy/gamma_m0), select a trial section from ISI handbook (SP 6), (2) design strength due to yielding of gross section Tdg = Ag fy / gamma_m0 (gamma_m0 = 1.10), (3) design strength due to rupture of critical section Tdn using Cl. 6.3.3 for angles (0.9 An fu / gamma_m1 + beta Ago fy / gamma_m0), with gamma_m1 = 1.25, (4) design strength due to block shear Tdb from Cl. 6.4, (5) net area calculation with bolt hole deduction (bolt dia + 2 mm), (6) governing design tension Td = min of above three, (7) efficiency = Td / Tu check (should be > 1 and close), (8) slenderness ratio check KL/r against 180 for tension with reversal or 400 otherwise (Table 3), (9) a connection note with number of M20 bolts 4.6 grade required. Format the output as an IS 800 stepwise design sheet with clause numbers. Consider these constraints: use SP 6(1) for section properties, single angle bolted on one leg requires shear lag factor beta from Cl. 6.3.3, and flag the governing failure mode.

prompts.title: Traffic Volume Study and PCU Calculation per IRC 106
prompts.prompt: Act as a transportation planning engineer with 10 years of experience conducting traffic surveys for metro feasibility studies in Pune and Ahmedabad. I need you to convert a classified traffic volume count into PCU/hour and determine service volume per IRC 106:1990. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my Transportation Engineering mini-project. I conducted a 1-hour classified volume count at a 4-lane urban arterial in [ENTER city]. Counts per hour: two-wheelers [ENTER], cars/taxis [ENTER], auto-rickshaws [ENTER], LCV [ENTER], bus [ENTER], truck 2-axle [ENTER], truck multi-axle [ENTER], cycles [ENTER]. Please provide: (1) a table of vehicle classes with counts and PCU equivalency factors from IRC 106 for urban roads, (2) PCU per hour for each class and total PCU/hr, (3) directional split assumption (60:40 peak), peak hour volume in one direction, (4) capacity of 4-lane urban arterial divided from IRC 106 Table (say 2400 PCU/hr/direction for undivided, 3600 for divided), (5) Volume to Capacity ratio, (6) Level of Service classification (A to F) based on V/C and speed, (7) peak hour factor PHF if 15-min counts available (assume 0.9 otherwise), (8) recommendations (signal coordination, lane addition, BRT) based on LOS, (9) a short note on how PCU factors differ between urban and rural per IRC 64 vs 106. Format the output as an analysis report with a classified volume table, a PCU conversion table and a LOS conclusion. Consider these constraints: follow IRC 106:1990 PCU values, use IRC 64:1990 for rural, and flag seasonal/daily adjustment needs.

prompts.title: Soil Classification: Grain Size, Atterberg, IS 1498
prompts.prompt: Act as a soil mechanics laboratory in-charge with 15 years of experience at IIT Bombay's geotechnical lab classifying soils for construction projects. I need you to classify a soil sample as per IS 1498:1970 (Indian Soil Classification System / ISCS which mirrors USCS with Indian modifications). Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student submitting my Soil Mechanics lab report. Sample data: sieve analysis — percentage passing 75 micron (silt+clay fraction) = [ENTER %], percentage passing 4.75 mm = [ENTER %], percentage passing 75 mm = 100. Atterberg limits on fraction passing 425 micron: liquid limit LL = [ENTER %], plastic limit PL = [ENTER %]. From sieve analysis: D10 = [ENTER mm], D30 = [ENTER mm], D60 = [ENTER mm]. Please provide: (1) gravel, sand, fines percentages with classification (coarse-grained if fines < 50%, fine-grained if >= 50%), (2) for coarse-grained: Cu = D60/D10, Cc = D30^2/(D10 x D60) with well-graded / poorly-graded criteria, (3) plasticity index PI = LL - PL, (4) A-line equation PI = 0.73(LL - 20) and U-line, plot point on plasticity chart (describe location), (5) final IS 1498 symbol (GW, GP, GM, GC, SW, SP, SM, SC, ML, CL, MI, CI, MH, CH, OL, OH, Pt), (6) engineering properties expected (permeability, compressibility, shear strength, suitability as subgrade/fill), (7) CBR range expected, (8) suitability for road subgrade/embankment/foundation. Format the output as a classification worksheet with final soil symbol boxed and a properties interpretation paragraph. Consider these constraints: use IS 1498:1970 (has additional MI and CI categories for intermediate plasticity 35-50), state all assumptions, and show the plasticity chart check calculation.

prompts.title: Compaction Proctor Test Analysis (IS 2720 Part 7)
prompts.prompt: Act as a highway materials engineer with 12 years of experience supervising embankment compaction for GMR and IRB highway projects. I need you to analyse Standard Proctor compaction test results and determine OMC and MDD. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student submitting my Soil Mechanics lab. Test data for 5 trials (mould volume 1000 cc, empty weight Wm kg): trial 1 to 5 — wet soil + mould mass [ENTER 5 values in g], water content from oven drying [ENTER 5 values in %]. Please provide: (1) bulk density rho for each trial = (wet mass - mould mass) / 1000 in g/cc, (2) dry density rho_d = rho / (1 + w/100) for each, (3) plotted compaction curve described in text with (w%, rho_d) points, (4) maximum dry density MDD and optimum moisture content OMC read from curve (peak), (5) zero air voids line rho_d_zav = Gs x rho_w / (1 + w x Gs) for Gs = 2.65 plotted alongside as check that no point crosses it, (6) degree of saturation at OMC, (7) comparison with modified Proctor expected values (typically MDD 5-10% higher, OMC 2-4% lower), (8) field density check: if 97% MDD required, state acceptable range, (9) CBR correlation expectation at OMC, (10) practical compaction note (roller type, layer thickness 200-250 mm, number of passes) for Indian highway embankment. Format the output as a test data table followed by interpretation and a boxed MDD-OMC result. Consider these constraints: follow IS 2720 Part 7 (standard) or Part 8 (modified), ensure no data point lies right of ZAV line (impossible), and flag outlier trials.

prompts.title: Seismic Base Shear per IS 1893 (Static Method)
prompts.prompt: Act as an earthquake engineering consultant with 18 years of experience designing buildings for seismic zones III-V across India, including Guwahati and Bhuj retrofits. I need you to compute the design seismic base shear for a regular RCC building using the equivalent static method of IS 1893 Part 1:2016. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my Earthquake Resistant Design assignment. Building: G+[ENTER storeys], floor-to-floor 3.0 m, plan [ENTER Lx x Ly in m], seismic zone [II/III/IV/V], soil type [I rocky / II medium / III soft], importance factor [1.0 residential / 1.2 important / 1.5 hospitals]. Lumped floor weights per storey [ENTER Wi in kN, typically 10-12 kN/m2 x plan area]. SMRF (response reduction R = 5) assumed. Please provide: (1) zone factor Z from Table 3 (0.10, 0.16, 0.24, 0.36), (2) fundamental natural period Ta using empirical formula 0.075 h^0.75 for RCC MRF without infill or 0.09 h/sqrt(d) with infill (state which), (3) spectral acceleration Sa/g from Figure 2 for given Ta and soil type, (4) horizontal seismic coefficient Ah = (Z/2) x (I/R) x (Sa/g), (5) seismic weight W = sum of Wi, (6) design base shear VB = Ah x W, (7) vertical distribution Qi = VB x Wi hi^2 / sum(Wi hi^2), (8) storey shear profile, (9) check drift limit 0.004 h per storey, (10) a note on when dynamic analysis becomes mandatory (height > 40 m in Zone IV-V or irregular). Format the output as a calculation sheet with tables for Ta, Sa/g, Ah, VB and vertical distribution. Consider these constraints: strictly follow IS 1893-1:2016 (not 2002), quote all clause and table numbers, and flag plan/vertical irregularities that would require dynamic analysis.

prompts.title: Wind Load Calculation per IS 875 Part 3
prompts.prompt: Act as a structural engineer with 15 years of experience designing tall structures and industrial buildings in coastal Chennai and cyclone-prone Odisha. I need you to compute design wind pressure on a rectangular RCC building using IS 875 Part 3:2015. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student. Building: height h = [ENTER in m], plan a x b = [ENTER in m], location [ENTER Indian city], terrain category [1 open/2 suburban/3 urban/4 city centre], topography flat, class of structure [A/B/C by largest dim], design life 50 years, importance general. Please provide: (1) basic wind speed Vb from Figure 1/Annex A for the city (e.g., Chennai 50 m/s, Mumbai 44 m/s, Delhi 47 m/s), (2) risk coefficient k1 from Table 1 (1.0 for general building, 50 yrs), (3) terrain and height factor k2 at several heights (10, 20, 30 m up to h) from Table 2 for the given terrain and class, (4) topography factor k3 (1.0 for flat), (5) importance factor k4 (1.0 general, 1.3 industrial), (6) design wind speed Vz = Vb k1 k2 k3 k4 at each height, (7) design wind pressure pz = 0.6 Vz^2 in N/m2, (8) design wind pressure pd = Kd Ka Kc pz with combination factor, area averaging factor, directionality factor, (9) external pressure coefficients Cpe on walls and roof from Table 5 (windward, leeward, sides) for h/w ratio, (10) internal pressure coefficient Cpi for permeability class, (11) net design pressure = (Cpe - Cpi) x pd on critical faces. Format the output as a height-wise wind pressure table followed by a pressure distribution sketch description on each face. Consider these constraints: use IS 875 Part 3:2015 (has new factors Kd, Ka, Kc), state the city's Vb from the zonal map, and note when dynamic analysis is required (Cl. 10, h/d > 5 etc.).

prompts.title: Prestressed Concrete Beam Design (Post-Tensioned)
prompts.prompt: Act as a prestressed concrete specialist with 20 years of experience designing PSC girders for metro viaducts and flyovers with L&T IDPL in Delhi and Nagpur. I need you to design a simply supported post-tensioned rectangular PSC beam for flexure using IS 1343:2012 Limit State Method. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my PSC elective assignment. Beam: span L = [ENTER in m], rectangular section b x D = [ENTER], M45 concrete, high tensile steel fpk = 1860 MPa (7-wire strands 12.7 mm), live load [ENTER in kN/m], finishes [ENTER], exposure mild. Please provide: (1) loads: self-weight, DL, LL, service and ultimate moments Ms and Mu at mid-span, (2) permissible stresses at transfer and service from IS 1343 Table 8 (ft_trans, fc_trans, ft_service, fc_service), (3) section modulus requirements Zt and Zb from the four stress inequalities (top/bottom at transfer/service) and check provided Z, (4) minimum prestressing force Pmin and eccentricity e from stress at bottom fibre at service, (5) number of strands (each with 100 kN effective), duct arrangement, (6) losses calculation — friction and anchorage slip (short term), elastic shortening, creep, shrinkage, relaxation (long term), assume total 18% loss, (7) ultimate flexural strength using Table 11 / strain compatibility for bonded PT, Mu,R vs Mu, (8) end block design note with bursting force, (9) deflection check at service (camber at transfer, net at service), (10) cable profile (parabolic with e_mid and e_support). Format the output as an LSM PSC design sheet with boxed values and clause references. Consider these constraints: follow IS 1343:2012 strictly, use high tensile strands fpk/1.15 for design strength, and flag Type 1/2/3 member classification.

prompts.title: Pile Foundation Capacity (Static Formula, IS 2911)
prompts.prompt: Act as a deep foundation engineer with 18 years of experience designing bored cast-in-situ piles for Mumbai Coastal Road and Chennai Metro projects. I need you to compute the ultimate and safe axial compression capacity of a bored cast-in-situ pile using static formula per IS 2911 Part 1 Sec 2. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. Pile details: diameter D = [ENTER in mm], length L = [ENTER in m] below cut-off level. Soil profile from borehole: layer 1 [ENTER depth, soil type, gamma, phi/c, N value]; layer 2 [...]; layer 3 (founding stratum) [...]. Water table at [ENTER depth]. Please provide: (1) shaft resistance Qs = sum over layers of (alpha c_bar + K sigma'_v tan delta) x As for each layer (use alpha for cohesive, K sigma' tan delta for cohesionless — state K from Table 1 and delta), (2) base resistance Qb = (cNc + sigma'v Nq + 0.5 gamma D Ngamma) x Ab, use Nc, Nq from IS 2911 Annex for bored piles (reduced from driven), (3) ultimate load capacity Qu = Qs + Qb, (4) safe load with FoS = 2.5 on Qu per IS 2911, (5) check against pile material capacity (0.25 fck x Ag for bored piles as per Cl. 5.11), (6) settlement check (elastic) Se = Qs L / (As Es) + Qb L / (Ab Eb), (7) negative skin friction consideration if soft clay layer present, (8) pile group capacity using efficiency factor or block failure check for group spacing 3D, (9) integrity and load test recommendation (initial load test on 1/500 piles, routine on 1/100 per IS 2911 Part 4). Format the output as a layered capacity calculation with summary. Consider these constraints: follow IS 2911 Part 1 Sec 2:2010, use bored pile reduction factors, and flag where Cu > 200 kPa alpha = 0.3.

prompts.title: Cantilever Retaining Wall Design per IS 14458
prompts.prompt: Act as a retaining structures specialist with 20 years of experience designing retaining walls for hill roads in Himachal Pradesh and flyover approaches for NHAI. I need you to design a RCC cantilever retaining wall using the working stress approach on stability and IS 456 LSM for section design. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. Wall retains earth of height H = [ENTER in m] above GL on heel side. Backfill: cohesionless gamma = 18 kN/m3, phi = 30 deg, horizontal top surface with surcharge [ENTER kN/m2]. Foundation soil: SBC = [ENTER kN/m2], coefficient of friction mu = 0.5. M25 concrete, Fe500. Please provide: (1) preliminary proportioning (base width = 0.5H to 0.7H, toe = B/3, heel = B/3 - thickness, stem thickness = H/12, base slab = H/10), (2) Rankine active earth pressure coefficient Ka = (1 - sin phi)/(1 + sin phi), pressure diagram, (3) forces: active thrust Pa = 0.5 gamma H^2 Ka, surcharge thrust, self-weight of stem, heel slab, toe slab, earth over heel, (4) stability check: factor of safety against sliding (>= 1.5), against overturning (>= 2.0), eccentricity check e <= B/6 to avoid tension, (5) base pressure pmax and pmin check against SBC, (6) stem design as vertical cantilever with Mu at base, thickness and Ast, (7) toe slab design with Mu at stem face, (8) heel slab design with net downward pressure, (9) shear key requirement if sliding FoS < 1.5, (10) detailing note with temperature and distribution steel, drainage through weep holes 100 mm @ 1 m c/c. Format the output as a stability sheet followed by section design. Consider these constraints: use IS 14458 Part 1/Part 2 for guidelines, minimum cover 50 mm for earth face, and show free body diagram in text.

prompts.title: Total Station Coordinate Computation (Setting Out)
prompts.prompt: Act as a setting out engineer with 12 years of experience using Leica and Topcon total stations for high-rise building layouts on Godrej Properties sites in Mumbai. I need you to compute the setting-out data (bearing and distance) from a total station occupation point to a set of design points, and prepare a stakeout list. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Advanced Surveying practical. Total station set up at control point CP1 with known coordinates N = [ENTER], E = [ENTER], backsight to CP2 with known coordinates N = [ENTER], E = [ENTER]. Design points to set out (building column grid intersections): [ENTER at least 6 points P1..P6 with design N, E, RL]. Instrument height HI = [ENTER], prism height HR = [ENTER]. Please provide: (1) a table of design points with Northing, Easting, RL, (2) for each point: delta N, delta E from CP1; horizontal distance sqrt(dN^2 + dE^2); bearing (azimuth) from atan2(dE, dN) in deg-min-sec converted to whole circle bearing, (3) reference backsight bearing to CP2 similarly, (4) horizontal angle to turn from backsight CP2 to each point = (bearing to point) - (bearing to CP2), adjusted to 0-360, (5) elevation difference = design RL - CP1 RL with a note on vertical angle/slope distance if instrument supports it, (6) sorted stakeout order (nearest first) to minimise walking, (7) a peg-list format output (Point ID, HA (DDD-MM-SS), HD (m), Delta Z (m)), (8) an accuracy note (angular 5" and distance +/-3mm+3ppm for typical total station). Format the output as a setting-out data sheet with a final peg list table. Consider these constraints: use grid north (not magnetic), show all angles in deg-min-sec, and note that on site always re-check backsight before stakeout.

prompts.title: GATE Civil Problem Solver with Step-by-Step Logic
prompts.prompt: Act as a GATE Civil Engineering coach with 15 years of experience at MADE EASY and ACE Engineering Academy, with AIR 12 yourself and 100+ students in top 100. I need you to solve a GATE-style civil engineering numerical problem with complete reasoning and flag traps. Here is the context: I am a civil engineering student preparing for GATE [ENTER year]. Here is the problem (paste exactly): [ENTER GATE question text with any given data and MCQ/NAT type]. Subject area: [ENTER SOM/RCC/Geotech/Fluid/Transport/Environment/Survey/Steel]. Please provide: (1) a one-line restatement of what is asked and what the unknown is, (2) the relevant formula or principle with the IS code / textbook reference, (3) listed knowns with units converted to SI, (4) a step-by-step numerical solution with unit tracking, (5) the final numerical answer to the precision expected (2-3 sig fig for NAT, closest option for MCQ), (6) dimensional/sanity check, (7) common GATE traps: sign error, wrong factor (L vs L/2, d vs D, radius vs diameter), wrong code edition, wrong units (kN vs N), (8) a 'what would the other options test' breakdown if MCQ, (9) a 30-second fast-solve shortcut for re-attempts, (10) one related concept worth revising and the PYQ year it last appeared. Format the output as an exam-style solution with the final answer boxed, followed by 'Trap & Tips' bullets. Consider these constraints: strictly use Indian conventions and IS codes, show all unit conversions, and label each step clearly.

prompts.title: Triaxial Shear Test Interpretation (UU, CU, CD)
prompts.prompt: Act as a soil mechanics professor with 22 years of experience running triaxial testing rigs at IIT Madras and advising on Chennai Metro tunnel projects. I need you to interpret triaxial shear test results and determine soil shear strength parameters. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student submitting my Soil Mechanics-2 lab. Test type: [ENTER UU unconsolidated undrained / CU consolidated undrained / CD consolidated drained]. Three specimens tested at cell pressures sigma3 = [ENTER 3 values in kPa], failure deviator stresses sigma1 - sigma3 = [ENTER 3 values in kPa], pore pressures at failure (if CU) u_f = [ENTER 3 values in kPa]. Soil is [ENTER saturated clay / dense sand / silty sand]. Please provide: (1) tabulated principal stresses sigma1, sigma3 (total) and sigma1', sigma3' (effective) for each specimen, (2) Mohr circles description (centre and radius) for all three, (3) total stress envelope: total cohesion c and total friction angle phi from tangent, (4) effective stress envelope c' and phi' (for CU/CD), (5) pore pressure parameter A at failure = delta u / delta sigma1 (for CU), (6) comparison with expected values (UU on saturated clay: phi_u = 0; CD on NC clay: c' = 0; CU on sand: phi' similar to direct shear), (7) p-q plot alternative with K_f line (a' and alpha'), conversion back to c' and phi' using tan phi' = sin alpha' etc., (8) selection of design parameters for given field condition (end of construction UU, long-term CD), (9) a note on test limitations. Format the output as an interpretation report with results table, Mohr circle description, and boxed c, phi values. Consider these constraints: follow IS 2720 Part 11 (UU) and Part 12 (CU), state sign convention, and flag if data is inconsistent (non-linear envelope).

prompts.title: Flow Over Rectangular Notch / Weir
prompts.prompt: Act as a fluid mechanics lab instructor with 15 years of experience running hydraulics labs at NIT Warangal. I need you to analyse discharge measurement over a rectangular notch and compute the coefficient of discharge Cd and actual discharge. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year civil student submitting my Fluid Mechanics lab report. Notch details: rectangular sharp-crested, crest width L = [ENTER in m], channel width B = [ENTER in m], crest height above channel bed P = [ENTER in m]. Five trials: head H over crest [ENTER 5 values in m], actual discharge measured by volumetric/weir tank [ENTER 5 values in m3/s or l/s], water temperature [ENTER degC]. Please provide: (1) theoretical discharge Qth = (2/3) Cd L sqrt(2g) H^(3/2) for each trial assuming Cd = 1, (2) Cd for each trial = Qactual / (2/3 L sqrt(2g) H^1.5), (3) mean Cd and standard deviation with typical range 0.60-0.64 for sharp-crested, (4) Francis formula Q = 1.84 (L - 0.1nH) H^1.5 comparison (n = number of end contractions, 0 for suppressed, 2 for contracted), (5) Rehbock formula Q = 1.78 Le He^1.5 with He = H + 0.0011, Le = L + 0.0026 check, (6) sources of error (approach velocity, viscosity, surface tension for small H, crest sharpness), (7) velocity of approach correction Va^2/2g and corrected H, (8) graph described in text log Q vs log H slope should be 1.5, (9) end-contraction effect if L < B, (10) a short note on converting to a broad-crested weir. Format the output as a lab-report-style analysis with data table, calculations, and conclusions. Consider these constraints: use g = 9.81 m/s2, state SI units, and flag any trial that deviates >5% from mean Cd.

prompts.title: Water Distribution Network Analysis (Hardy Cross)
prompts.prompt: Act as an environmental engineer with 12 years of experience designing water supply networks for Jal Jeevan Mission schemes in Rajasthan villages. I need you to analyse a pipe network using the Hardy Cross method and compute corrected flows. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my Environmental Engineering-1 assignment. Network: two-loop pipe network with [ENTER pipe details: pipe ID, length L in m, diameter D in mm, Hazen-Williams C]. Inflow at node [ENTER] = [ENTER Q in l/s], outflows at nodes [ENTER with quantities]. Assume initial flows that satisfy continuity at every node. Please provide: (1) pipe data table with L, D, C, (2) Hazen-Williams K for each pipe = 10.67 L / (C^1.852 x D^4.87) (SI, Q in m3/s, hf in m), (3) loop-wise assumed flows satisfying continuity (show at each node), (4) iteration 1: for each loop compute sum(hf) = sum(K Q^1.852) with signs (clockwise positive) and sum(1.852 K |Q|^0.852), correction delta Q = -sum(hf) / sum(1.852 K |Q|^0.852), (5) apply correction to each pipe in the loop (common pipes get correction from both loops with appropriate sign), (6) iterate until delta Q < 1% in both loops (usually 3-4 iterations), (7) final flows and head losses per pipe, (8) node pressure heads starting from a reservoir at known HGL, (9) velocity check (0.6-2 m/s typical), (10) identify bottleneck pipe. Format the output as a per-iteration table for each loop followed by final results. Consider these constraints: use Hazen-Williams (Indian design manual CPHEEO), state units, and flag if any velocity exceeds 2.5 m/s (needs resizing).

prompts.title: Steel Beam Design (Laterally Supported) per IS 800
prompts.prompt: Act as a steel structures designer with 18 years of experience designing industrial steel buildings for Jindal Steel and BHEL workshops. I need you to design a laterally supported rolled steel beam using IS 800:2007 LSM. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Design of Steel Structures assignment. Beam: simply supported span L = [ENTER in m], laterally restrained by RCC slab on top flange. Factored UDL wu = [ENTER in kN/m] including self-weight guess. Steel grade Fe410. Try an ISMB/ISWB section. Please provide: (1) design BM Mu = wu L^2 / 8 and SF Vu = wu L / 2, (2) required plastic section modulus Zpz = Mu x gamma_m0 / fy with gamma_m0 = 1.10, (3) trial section from SP 6(1) (ISMB 400 / ISWB 450 etc.) with properties Zpz, Zez, Iz, bf, tf, tw, d, (4) classification (plastic/compact/semi-compact) using b/tf and d/tw limits from Table 2, (5) design bending strength Md = beta_b Zpz fy / gamma_m0 with beta_b = 1.0 for plastic, with check Md <= 1.2 Ze fy / gamma_m0 to prevent yielding at service, (6) shear strength Vd = (Av fy / sqrt(3)) / gamma_m0 where Av = h tw, check Vu < 0.6 Vd (low shear), (7) deflection check delta = 5 wL^4 / 384 EI against L/300 for general / L/360 for brittle finish, (8) web buckling and crippling check at support, (9) a bearing stiffener check if needed, (10) efficiency summary. Format the output as a steel design sheet with clause numbers and a final section call-out. Consider these constraints: use SP 6(1) section tables, IS 800:2007 Cl. 8 for bending and Cl. 8.4 for shear, and flag if serviceability deflection governs over strength.

prompts.title: Contour Map and Earthwork Volume (Grid Method)
prompts.prompt: Act as a land survey engineer with 14 years of experience preparing site grading plans for SEZ plots in Sri City and Oragadam near Chennai. I need you to compute earthwork cut and fill volumes from grid level data using the prismoidal and average end area methods. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Surveying-2 practical. Rectangular plot divided into [ENTER m x n] grid of squares each of side a = [ENTER in m]. Existing ground RLs at each grid node: [ENTER grid of RLs row by row]. Proposed formation level [ENTER single level or grading slope]. Please provide: (1) a grid map described in text with existing RLs, (2) cut or fill at each node = existing RL - formation RL (positive cut, negative fill), (3) volume by average of four corners method for each square Vi = (a^2/4) x (h1 + h2 + h3 + h4), summed for total cut and fill, (4) alternative: Simpson's rule for trapezoidal sections in one direction as check, (5) border nodes weighted 1, edge nodes 2, interior 4 in the unit-area method V = (a^2/4) x (sum h1 + 2 sum h2 + 3 sum h3 + 4 sum h4) for mixed patterns, (6) total cut in m3, total fill in m3, net balance with haul direction, (7) shrinkage/swell factor application (typical 10% shrinkage for fill placed compacted), (8) transition line where cut changes to fill (zero contour), (9) mass haul diagram concept note, (10) cost implication at Rs [ENTER per m3]. Format the output as grid tables followed by volume summary. Consider these constraints: state assumption if formation is sloped, use metric units, and round volumes to nearest m3.

prompts.title: Viva and Interview Q&A for RCC Design
prompts.prompt: Act as an external examiner and HR technical interviewer with 25 years of experience in RCC design at AECOM India and STUP Consultants, having conducted 500+ B.Tech vivas and 300+ L1 GET interviews. I need you to prepare me for a viva voce / technical interview on RCC design covering IS 456. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil engineering student with a project on [ENTER your project title] and I have an upcoming [ENTER end-sem viva / campus interview for L&T/Tata Projects/AECOM/GET role]. Please provide: (1) 25 viva/interview questions on IS 456 RCC design, arranged from fundamental to tough, covering: working stress vs limit state, partial safety factors, stress block parameters, balanced/under/over-reinforced sections, Mu,lim coefficients for Fe415 vs Fe500, minimum and maximum steel limits, development length, shear design and tau_c table, torsion equivalent shear and moment, deflection control, crack width, cover requirements, durability per Table 3, detailing rules (stirrup spacing, lap length, curtailment), (2) for each question: a concise model answer (3-6 lines) in a confident student voice, (3) the exact IS 456 clause number to quote for credibility, (4) a 'trap follow-up' question the examiner is likely to ask as a drill-down, (5) three common mistakes students make answering that question, (6) a short 60-second elevator pitch on my project linking to RCC fundamentals, (7) five resume-line bullets summarising RCC competency, (8) quick-reference flashcard of numbers to memorise (Mu,lim/bd^2 for Fe415 M20 = 2.76 etc.). Format the output as a numbered Q&A with each item having Question / Model Answer / Clause / Trap Follow-up / Mistakes. Consider these constraints: use only IS 456:2000, Indian textbook terminology (Pillai-Menon, Unnikrishnan, Varghese), and write as if coaching a student for a panel viva.

prompts.title: Civil Engineering Final Year Project Proposal
prompts.prompt: Act as a PhD civil engineering professor with 18 years of experience guiding B.Tech final year projects at an Indian university (NIT / IIT / Anna University). I need you to help me structure a compelling final year project proposal that will impress my HOD review panel and potentially lead to a journal paper. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student. My area of interest is [ENTER one of: structural, geotechnical, transportation, water resources, construction management, environmental]. Preliminary idea: [ENTER 2-3 lines describing your raw idea]. Resources available: [ENTER lab equipment, software like STAAD/ETABS/PLAXIS/AutoCAD, budget Rs X, team size]. Duration: [ENTER months]. Please provide: (1) a refined, specific, and defensible project title (under 15 words) with Indian context relevance, (2) abstract (150 words) with clear objective, methodology, and expected outcome, (3) literature gap (3 bullets citing Indian/international context, e.g., ACI, IS codes, IRC, recent 2022-2024 journal themes), (4) 3-5 SMART objectives, (5) methodology as a weekly Gantt chart broken into phases: literature review, experimental/analytical setup, data collection/simulation, analysis, report writing, (6) expected results and how they will be validated, (7) required resources and budget estimate in INR, (8) risk register with 3 risks and mitigations, (9) relevance to national missions (Smart Cities, PMAY, Bharatmala, Jal Jeevan, Gati Shakti), (10) publication plan (which Indian/international journal or conference — ICI, ISET, ICGE, IGC). Format the output as a university-style project proposal document with section headings. Consider these constraints: feasible for B.Tech scope, aligned with Indian construction context, and include at least one IS code reference relevant to the topic.

prompts.title: Construction Project Schedule with CPM Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a planning engineer with 15 years of experience at Shapoorji Pallonji using Primavera P6 for high-rise construction in Mumbai and Dubai. I need you to prepare a Critical Path Method (CPM) network analysis and Gantt chart for a construction activity sequence. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student doing my Construction Management assignment. Project: [ENTER construction type, e.g., G+3 residential building shell, 500 m2 plot]. Activities with durations and dependencies: [ENTER activity list, e.g., A Site clearance 5d; B Excavation 10d after A; C PCC 3d after B; D Footings 12d after C; E Columns 15d after D; F Plinth beam 7d after D; G Brickwork 20d after E+F; ...]. Please provide: (1) a clean activity-on-node (AON) network table with predecessors and durations, (2) forward pass: ES and EF for each activity, (3) backward pass: LS and LF, (4) total float TF = LS - ES = LF - EF for each activity, (5) free float FF where applicable, (6) identification of the critical path (activities with TF = 0) and project duration, (7) a Gantt chart described as a text table with weeks on columns and activities on rows, shading working span, highlighting critical path, (8) resource assumption (mason, mazdoor, carpenter, bar bender crew sizes), (9) resource levelling note if any over-allocation, (10) PERT extension note with optimistic/most-likely/pessimistic if uncertainty is high, (11) recommended monitoring points (S-curve, EVM basics). Format the output as a CPM workbook with forward/backward pass tables, critical path listed, and a Gantt description. Consider these constraints: use Indian site crew rates and working 6-day weeks, flag monsoon June-Sept impact if duration exceeds 3 months, and align with typical CPWD milestone billing.

prompts.title: Open Channel Design: Manning's Equation
prompts.prompt: Act as a hydraulic engineer with 14 years of experience designing irrigation canals for Narmada Valley Development Authority and drainage channels for Bengaluru's stormwater network. I need you to design an open channel (trapezoidal) to carry a given design discharge using Manning's equation and check for best hydraulic section. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year civil student doing my Hydraulic Engineering assignment. Design discharge Q = [ENTER in m3/s], longitudinal slope S = [ENTER as decimal e.g., 0.0002], lining [earthen/concrete/brick] with Manning's n = [ENTER n], side slopes z = [ENTER e.g., 1.5H:1V]. Please provide: (1) Manning's equation Q = (1/n) A R^(2/3) S^(1/2), (2) area A = (b + zy) y and wetted perimeter P = b + 2y sqrt(1+z^2), hydraulic radius R = A/P, (3) design by iteration/assumption of either bed width b or depth y — use best hydraulic section for trapezoidal: b = 2y(sqrt(1+z^2) - z) and R = y/2, solve for y then b, (4) computed A, P, R and verify Q using Manning, (5) velocity V = Q/A check against permissible (non-scour) velocity for the lining (0.6 m/s earthen, 2.5 m/s concrete), (6) Froude number Fr = V / sqrt(g A / T) with top width T = b + 2zy, state sub-critical / critical / supercritical, (7) freeboard per IS 7112 (typical 0.5-1.0 m), (8) final section dimensions with 150 mm freeboard margin added to y, (9) a note on lining thickness and cost-per-metre implications, (10) end-of-channel transition note. Format the output as a design sheet with equations, iterations, and a boxed final section. Consider these constraints: follow IS 7112 for lined canals and IS 10430 for canal layout, state unit of each variable, and flag if Fr is near 1 (unstable).

prompts.title: Building Plan Review against NBC 2016
prompts.prompt: Act as a building plan sanction officer at a Municipal Corporation in Bengaluru (BBMP) with 15 years of experience reviewing residential plans against the National Building Code of India 2016 and local bye-laws. I need you to review a draft residential building plan and flag compliance issues. Here is the context: I am a final-year civil student submitting my Building Planning and Drawing project and want to make sure the design clears a plan-sanction review. Plot details: plot area [ENTER in sqm], plot dimensions [ENTER L x B], road abutting [ENTER width], type [ENTER residential G+X]. Proposed design: setbacks front [ENTER], rear [ENTER], sides [ENTER], FSI/FAR used [ENTER], ground coverage [ENTER %], building height [ENTER], number of dwelling units [ENTER], staircase width [ENTER], corridor [ENTER], room sizes [ENTER list of rooms with dimensions], kitchen [ENTER], toilet [ENTER], parking provided [ENTER ECS], setback for fire tender [ENTER]. Please provide: (1) a checklist table with columns: NBC 2016 Part 3 clause, requirement, my provision, compliance status (OK / Violation / Marginal), (2) setback compliance vs local bye-laws (Karnataka Town and Country Planning Act) for the given plot and road width, (3) FAR/ground coverage check, (4) minimum room area and ventilation check (habitable 9.5 sqm, kitchen 5.5 sqm, toilet 1.8 sqm), (5) staircase and width check (min 1.0 m for residential, headroom 2.1 m, riser/tread), (6) natural light and ventilation openable area 1/10 of floor area, (7) parking ECS per dwelling unit per BBMP bye-laws, (8) fire safety: refuge area for height > 15 m, two staircases for G+3 and above, fire tender access, (9) barrier-free/accessibility per NBC Part 3 Cl. 13 (ramps 1:12), (10) septic tank / STP need, (11) top 5 action items to fix before submission. Format the output as an official-style plan scrutiny report. Consider these constraints: cite NBC 2016 Part 3 and Part 4 (fire) clauses, include BBMP/Karnataka bye-law references, and write in a formal scrutiny tone.
